Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, honestly. I'd block out time in the evenings after work, usually around 22 hours a week total. Being 27 and juggling responsibilities, I needed structure or nothing would get done. I'd listen to lectures while commuting or doing chores since I learn better by hearing things. The routine was there, but whether I was actually absorbing everything is another question entirely.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but life has a way of disrupting plans. Early in the week I'd be disciplined, but by Thursday or Friday, exhaustion from work would catch up. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram more hours in, which threw off my whole rhythm. The pattern was supposed to be steady, but it ended up being more reactive than I wanted it to be.
Q3: Mostly I logged in because I knew I was supposed to. It became this checkbox thing—log in, see what's due, maybe watch a video. Sometimes I'd get notifications about discussion posts from classmates and that would pull me back in. Fear of missing something important was a big motivator, honestly. I was scared of falling behind without realizing it.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, logging in did become automatic. I'd wake up, check my phone, then check the LMS before breakfast. It felt like part of my morning routine alongside checking emails. But then I hit a rough patch with some personal stuff and the habit broke. I never quite got back to that automatic feeling after that.
Q5: The recorded lectures were essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could replay sections I didn't catch the first time, which helped a lot. The discussion boards were also useful because hearing other students' perspectives through their written posts—I'd almost read them out loud to myself—helped concepts click. Those two things made me feel like I was actually in a class, not just staring at a screen.
Q6: The reading materials and PDFs, I barely touched those. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things, and reading long documents on a screen just doesn't work for my brain. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd sit in a folder untouched. The supplementary links to external websites also got ignored because I didn't have unlimited data and couldn't always access them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could hear it or if it directly connected to an assignment. If I could listen to something while doing other tasks, that was golden. Activities that felt disconnected from assessments or that required expensive textbooks I didn't have—those felt like a waste. I needed to see the practical payoff given how limited my time and resources were.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're working, studying, and trying to keep your head above water financially, optional means skippable. Required activities got done because they had to, even if I was exhausted. There was guilt around the optional stuff, but survival mode meant prioritizing what counted toward my grade.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that the LMS would never capture. I'd download audio files of lectures and listen to them on repeat during my commute. I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos explaining the same topics in different ways. My phone's voice memo app was basically my second study tool.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was deeply engaged with course material but barely touched the LMS. I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while working, discussing concepts with a coworker who was also taking classes, or verbally quizzing myself during breaks. None of that shows up anywhere. The LMS only saw me when I physically logged in, which wasn't always when I was learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio if I could, and then listen to it maybe five or six more times offline. Same with any materials I could convert to audio format. One login, but hours of actual engagement. The LMS would show one view, but that material was running through my earbuds for days afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. Work increased my hours, I was stressed about money, and I started feeling scared that I was going to fail despite trying. The CBT sessions I attended helped me manage the anxiety somewhat, but the fear was still there underneath. When you're scared and exhausted, even logging in feels like climbing a mountain.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I'd sometimes have to choose between studying and eating properly or getting enough sleep. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely because I didn't have extra money for tutoring or supplementary materials.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open a video, let it play, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about work drama, just zoning out. Technically I was 'watching' the content, but I wasn't there mentally. My watch time might look decent, but the actual learning during those sessions was minimal.
Q15: I postponed the written assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Writing is harder for me than listening, and I'd convince myself I'd do better if I waited until I understood the material more. But then deadlines would hit and I'd rush through something subpar. Fear of doing poorly made me avoid starting, which guaranteed I'd do poorly. Vicious cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire engagement strategy. I'd check the LMS specifically to see when things were due and work backward from there. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all with certain modules. The pressure was necessary but also exhausting. Everything became about the deadline rather than actual learning.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it boosted my mood and made me want to engage more. But negative feedback, even constructive criticism, would send me spiraling a bit. I'd avoid logging in for a day or two after getting a bad grade back. The CBT helped me process that better over time, but initially feedback felt dangerous.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which actually helped someone like me who needs routine. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework. But the structure also assumed everyone had equal access to resources and time, which wasn't true for me. When I fell behind, the structure felt punishing rather than supportive.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier for my learning style. What made it harder was the heavy reliance on reading materials and the assumption that everyone could access external resources easily. Discussion posts helped because I could engage with peers, but the interface wasn't always intuitive. More flexibility in how to demonstrate learning would have helped.
Q20: I think that happens when people are trying but struggling. You log in repeatedly hoping something will click, you watch videos searching for understanding, you read discussion posts looking for help. But if the material isn't landing or you're too stressed to produce work, all that activity doesn't translate to submissions. Activity can mean effort without results.
Q21: That makes sense to me because real learning doesn't always happen on the platform. Someone could attend study groups, use textbooks, listen to podcasts, or have conversations that deepen understanding. If they're efficient when they do log in, they might not need much LMS time. I wish I'd been more like that, honestly. My activity was high but scattered.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you logged in, how long you spent, what you clicked on. But they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't tell if you were focused or distracted, scared or confident, learning deeply or just going through motions. They definitely miss all the offline work that auditory learners like me rely on heavily.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when you think about concepts outside of study time, when you want to discuss ideas with others. For me, it's when I'm actively listening and processing, not just letting audio play in the background. True engagement is mental presence, not just physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the semester, or how hard I was actually trying despite my grades. It wouldn't capture the hours of listening to lectures on repeat during my commute, or the discussions with coworkers about course concepts. It wouldn't show that I attended CBT sessions to manage my stress about the course. My engagement was real, even if the data doesn't reflect it.
